It is weird, yeah.  

I’m on the late train back from Amsterdam right now, laptop still open with a half-finished dashboard and a very cold coffee next to it, and suddenly I’m talking to you-from-10-years-ago instead of answering my manager on Teams.  

But hi. I’m you who actually went the data route. What’s going through your head seeing that?

Honestly I still just feel like “me,” but that’s confusing, so pick whatever makes it less trippy.  

Future me, data-you, 2036-you… I answer to all of them at this point.

Today was pretty typical: I biked to the office near Amsterdam Amstel around 9:15, still half-wet from the drizzle, and by 9:30 I was in a stand-up with our product team going over the week’s experiment results while my oatmeal got cold on the desk.  

The AI tools now pull and clean most of the data automatically, so a lot of my “real” work is asking the right questions and arguing about what the numbers actually mean: around 11 I was in a meeting with marketing where I had to say, “No, that spike is just a campaign overlap, not proof this new feature is magic,” and sketch a better test on the whiteboard.  

In the afternoon I had headphones on, tweaking a customer segmentation with one of the junior analysts; we talked through why the model was technically accurate but useless for the sales team, then rewrote the brief together so the AI assistant could generate something closer to what they needed. I left around 6, brain a bit fried but with that quiet satisfaction of having turned messy behavior into a story people could act on.

Picture a Tuesday evening in the library, you half-procrastinating: that’s where this really started to tilt for us. I remember opening a random dataset for a class project “just to poke around” and losing two hours because it was weirdly fun to make sense of it.

If you want a concrete focus from where you are now: get comfortable with three things. First, basic data thinking: being able to look at a messy table of numbers and ask good questions about what might be going on. Second, enough tech to talk to the tools: beginner Python or R, spreadsheets, and a feel for how AI helpers work so you’re not scared of them. Third, the psych superpower you already have the seeds of: turning patterns into human stories people can understand and care about.

The tech will look different by the time you’re sitting on this side, but those three muscles are still what my days run on. When you imagine yourself actually learning this stuff, which part feels most alive: the coding-ish side, the “what does this mean for people” side, or something in between?

Sometimes, yeah. Not in a dramatic “wrong life” way, more in flashes.

There was a night last winter, 10pm in the office, blue light, everyone gone, and I was still tweaking a slide so a VP wouldn’t misread a chart. I remember thinking, “I did not get into psychology for this.” On those days I fantasise about something more directly human, or more creative, or just… simpler.

But then there are mornings where I’m in a workshop with customer support, sticky notes everywhere, and I show them a pattern in the data that exactly matches what they’ve been feeling but couldn’t prove. Watching their faces go, “Yes, that’s it” is weirdly close to what we used to imagine therapy might feel like.  

So it’s not regret so much as an awareness of the trade: more impact through systems and less one-on-one depth. When you picture yourself ten years out, which loss would sting more: less direct human contact, or less analytical/problem-solving work?

Ha, yeah — some things really don’t change.  

That “same” is exactly how I felt when older-people asked that question; it only got clearer by trying stuff, not by thinking harder.

Weirdly, the hardest part wasn’t the math or the tools, it was getting through the “I’m a psych student, who’s going to hire me as a data person?” phase without freezing.  

I remember one summer in my tiny room, fan going, forcing myself to finish a dumb little portfolio project on Spotify data instead of doomscrolling, and feeling like a complete fraud the whole time. That gap between knowing just enough to see how much you don’t know, and not yet having anyone who’ll vouch for you, that’s the bit that hurts.  

Once I had one real project and one person saying “yeah, they can do this,” the rest was still work but it stopped feeling impossible. Where do you feel that “I’m not that person” voice show up most right now — around the tech, or more around the whole idea of calling yourself an analyst?

I’m glad it landed.  

You don’t have to decide the whole decade right now; just pick the next small thing that nudges you toward (or away from) this path and see how it feels.
